summary: Stancil argues that as LLMs proliferate, they will sort into “low-skill” and “high-skill” tiers much like human labor markets, with cost and capability gaps driving differentiation. This could create new forms of inequality and competitive advantage, as premium models compound small advantages over time, even if baseline tools become cheap and widespread.
money_quotes: The introduction of Dolly suggests that LLMs and other generative AI models are also “skilled,” and could be divided along the same crude lines that economists use to divide the labor force.
These high-skill models are likely to be more expensive to employ than their less-skilled counterparts.
Employing a marginally better AI “workforce” might not make a discernible difference in how a company operates from day to day, but those benefits could accrue into huge gaps over time.
The reality, however, seems much more complicated—and much weirder.
Whatever happens, it feels like one thing is pretty clear: We’re barreling towards a very strange future.
key_insight: LLMs are likely to form stratified labor markets where marginal capability differences create compounding economic and social advantages.
